Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF FUNG LEE AS PERSONAL REPRESENTATIVE OF THE ESTATE OF GIM LEE, And ALBERT LEE's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings Directed At Defendants Affirmative Defenses [Non-Statutory Jop Motion].

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's "motion for judgment on the pleadings directed at defendants' affirmative defenses" is denied. The affirmative defenses are in answers filed back in September 2023. (See Opp. 1:19-25.) Trial is now less than a month away. As a leading treatise states: "Trial judges are often reluctant to grant last minute challenges to the pleadings because they disrupt the court's trial calendar and may result in unnecessary trial preparation expenses for both sides." (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 7:287.)

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, with a cover letter stating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative Summary Adjudication, Of Defendants
Off Calendar Oct-10-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Atty. ALBERT LEE, Esq. (tel: 415-215-6868) for Plaintiff.
Atty. ROBYN ROBERTS, Esq. of Donnelly Nelson & Depolo, APC (tel: 925-287-8181) for Defendants (via Zoom videoconference).

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, AND ANNE M. SUSKIND, M.D.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative Summary Adjudication, Of Defendants.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant's motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied. Defendant failed to maintain its initial burden of production per CCP 437(p)(2).

The court sustains plaintiff's objections to exhibit 1 to the Stayduhar declaration (purported medical records) on the grounds of hearsay and lack of authentication.

"Although hospital and medical records are hearsay, they can be admitted under the business records exception to the hearsay rule. [citations] Such records, however, must be properly authenticated." (Garibay v. Hemmat (2008) 161 Cal.App.4th 735, 742.) "Without those hospital records, and without testimony providing for authentication of such records, [the expert] declaration had no evidentiary basis." (Id.)

Here, the alleged medical records are attached to the declaration of counsel Stayduhar. She merely states: "I certify that the documents attached hereto as Exhibit 1 are true and correct copies of medical records pertaining to decedent Gim Lee from UCSF medical Center." (Stayduhar Decl., par. 2.) Counsel fails to demonstrate any personal knowledge for this assertion and does not authenticate the proffered medical records.

Also attached to this declaration is a custodian of record declaration. That declaration does not fill the evidentiary gulf. The declaration does not comply with Evidence Code section 1561(a)(3) and (5) because it does not set forth "[a] description of the mode of preparation of the records" nor that "[t]he records were prepared by the personnel of the business in the ordinary course of business." There is insufficient evidence to qualify the attachments as business records excepted from the hearsay rule. (Evidence Code sec. 1271.) The court further notes that the custodian declaration does not meet the subscription requirement of CCP 2015.5. The declaration was not made "under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California" nor is it clear where the declaration was signed.

Given all these evidentiary failings, the motion is denied in its entirety.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (302/RBU)
